ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bento food product class; no specific salmon-and-teriyaki-chicken bento entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box portion is approximately 400 g, comprising steamed white rice, a grilled salmon fillet portion, teriyaki-glazed chicken, and assorted vegetable sides.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon and Teriyaki Chicken Bento — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salmon and Teriyaki Chicken Bento (cr_id F05100116). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (grilled salmon + teriyaki chicken + white rice) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; White rice (steamed, Japanese-style) GI reference; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite bento dish. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The protein and fat components (salmon, teriyaki chicken) lower the effective meal GI relative to plain rice. A conservative mid-range value of 64 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 89.6) / 100 ≈ 57.3, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 22.4 × 4 = 89.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 246 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 984 mg sodium, representing ~43% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request teriyaki sauce on the side or ask for reduced-sodium preparation, as a full bento serving contributes nearly half the recommended daily sodium intake; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 22.4 g/100 g from white rice (GI ~64) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) produces a high glycemic load per full serving (~57 GL units), risking post-prandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to half, substitute brown rice where available, and increase the salmon and vegetable portions to lower the meal glycemic load and improve the carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); total fat is moderate at 3.81 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~0.9 g/100 g; sodium is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This bento is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to the omega-3 content from salmon, but individuals with CVD should limit portion size or request reduced teriyaki sauce to keep sodium intake within the 1,500–2,000 mg/day cardiovascular guideline.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon is a primary dietary source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, which have clinically demonstrated anti-inflammatory effects on joint tissue.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This bento is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request reduced teriyaki sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling, and balance the white rice with additional salmon and vegetable portions to limit refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon and Teriyaki Chicken Bento is a Japanese-style composite meal served in a compartmentalised bento box, consisting of steamed white rice, a grilled salmon fillet, and teriyaki-glazed chicken alongside assorted vegetable sides. The salmon is typically grilled or pan-seared to achieve a lightly crisp exterior with a moist, flaky interior, while the chicken is coated in a sweet-savoury teriyaki glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, then grilled or broiled. The rice base is plain steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 149 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 3.81 g fat, 22.4 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 246 mg sodium. The salmon component contributes cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making this bento nutritionally superior to chicken-only or pork-based bento options. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) based on the white rice component, and a full 400 g serving carries a high glycemic load (~57 GL units), which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is borderline at 246 mg/100 g, primarily from the teriyaki sauce and soy-based seasoning, requiring caution for hypertensive individuals. The dish is halal-certified in many Singapore food court and hawker settings and is commonly consumed as a working lunch. It is sold widely in Japanese food courts, supermarket ready-meal sections, and fast-casual Japanese restaurant chains across Singapore.

How many calories in a salmon and teriyaki chicken bento?
Is bento box good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100116) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the HPB canonical database. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the dominant white rice component, which is the standard defensible approach for composite rice-based dishes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (grilled salmon ~0.5 g/100 g sat fat + teriyaki chicken ~1.2 g/100 g sat fat + white rice ~0.1 g/100 g sat fat, weighted by approximate component proportions ~30% protein components, 50% rice, 20% vegetables); HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this composite bento dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact formulation as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for composite bento dishes; potassium_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; cholesterol_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — full micronutrient panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — trace mineral panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this composite dish. Source consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) as of April 2026. Limited published data for traditional preparation of composite bento boxes commonly focuses on macronutrients and key minerals only. 3. GI value of 64 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite bento exists in the approved GI literature. 4. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.318 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is a forbidden/unsupported source and has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. 5. Halal dietary tag preserved from input; kosher not confirmed. 6. The food is Japanese cuisine; mandatory Japanese local-language name is provided. Chinese names are provided as supplementary aliases relevant to the Singapore food context. 7. Aliases consistency self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — PASSED.
